title: Catan
resolution_core: 2d6 production rolls + open trading; build to 10 victory points

vector_explanations.economy.production.dice_resource_generat: Every turn opens with a 2d6 roll; each terrain hex whose number token matches pays its resource to all adjacent buildings, one card per settlement and two per city.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resource_sharing: Production rolls pay everyone at once: a rolled 9 yields cards to every player with a settlement or city touching a 9-hex, not just the roller, so opponents profit from your turn.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.triggered_income: Income arrives off-turn whenever anyone rolls your numbers, so placing on frequently rolled intersections builds a passive engine that runs during all four players' turns.
vector_explanations.economy.barter.commodity_money: There is no cash: brick, lumber, wool, grain and ore are paid directly in fixed recipes, such as brick plus lumber for a road or three ore plus two grain for a city.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.barter: Domestic trade lets the active player swap resource cards directly with any opponent at whatever ratio both accept; goods-for-goods exchange is the game's main liquidity valve.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.barter_negotiation: Trade terms are haggled openly, but only with the player whose turn it is, and pure gifts are forbidden; every deal must move at least one card in each direction.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.exchange_rates: The bank always trades 4:1, generic harbors improve that to 3:1, and resource-specific harbors give 2:1 in their good, so settling ports upgrades your exchange rate permanently.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.finite_supply: Each resource has exactly 19 cards in the bank; if a roll's production cannot be fully paid to two or more players, that resource is simply not distributed that roll.
vector_explanations.politics.intrigue.robber: On a 7 no one produces: players holding more than seven cards discard half, and the roller moves the robber to blockade one hex and steals a random card from an adjacent player.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: The seven-card threshold is a soft hand limit enforced by the robber: whenever a 7 is rolled, anyone with eight or more resource cards must discard half of them, rounded down.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Resource cards are held facing yourself; because gains are public but spends and trades blur the picture, robber steals and embargo decisions rely on reading hidden hands.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.longest_road: The Longest Road card is worth 2 VP to whoever maintains a continuous road of five or more segments, and it changes hands the moment a rival builds a strictly longer one.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.road_river_connecti: Roads are built on hex edges and new settlements must connect to your own road network while honoring the distance rule of two intersections between settlements.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.land_grab_expansion: Intersections are claimed first come, first served, and the distance rule makes each settlement radiate a blocked zone, so early road races decide who gets the last good spots.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.starting_position_draf: Setup is a structured draft of two settlements with attached roads; your second settlement immediately pays one resource card for each hex it touches, seeding your opening hand.
vector_explanations.turns.order.snake_order: Initial placement runs 1-2-3-4 then reverses 4-3-2-1, so the last-seated player places twice in a row as compensation for choosing last in the first pass.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.modular_boards: The island is assembled from 19 shuffled terrain hexes ringed by harbor pieces, so the geography and port layout are different in every game under identical rules.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Both the terrain hexes and the numbered production tokens are laid out variably at setup, changing which resources are scarce and which intersections are premium each play.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.transparent_distributions: Number tokens print pip dots showing each value's 2d6 frequency (five pips on 6 and 8, one on 2 and 12), making the exact production odds of every intersection public.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.probability_management: Skilled placement is odds arithmetic: summing pips across a settlement's three hexes, diversifying number coverage, and avoiding correlated numbers that go cold together.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: The game ends instantly when a player reaches 10 victory points on their own turn, counting buildings, Longest Road, Largest Army, and hidden VP development cards.

title: Carcassonne
resolution_core: draw-and-place tile laying with meeple claims on emerging features

vector_explanations.spatial.placement.tile_laying_matching: Each drawn landscape tile must be placed so every touching edge continues what it meets: city edges against city, road against road, field against field.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.tile_rotation_orientat: Tiles may be rotated to any of four orientations before placement, and rotation choice decides which features connect, extend, or are deliberately walled off.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.terrain_placement_rest: A tile can only be added edge-to-edge to the existing layout, never diagonally or as an island; if a drawn tile fits nowhere it is discarded and a new one drawn.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.feature_completion: Closing a feature scores it at once and frees its meeples: a completed city pays two points per tile plus two per pennant, a finished road one point per tile, a cloister nine.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.adjacency_bonuses: A cloister's value is set entirely by its neighborhood: it scores one point for its own tile plus one for each of the up to eight tiles surrounding it.
vector_explanations.spatial.geometry.adjacency_definitions: The rules define adjacency per feature type: cloisters count all eight surrounding tiles including diagonals, while roads, cities and fields connect only through matching edges.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.tile_placement_roads: The game is the canonical roads-and-cities tile layer: every placement extends shared roads, city segments, fields, or cloisters that score when their feature is finished.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.region_scoring: Fields are bounded regions scored only at game end, paying their majority farmer three points per completed city touching the field, which makes farming a long-horizon land war.
vector_explanations.spatial.area.area_control_majority: When features merge through tile placement, all meeples on the combined feature are compared; the plurality holder scores it all, and tied players each score full value.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.meeple_majority: Control of every road, city and field is decided purely by counting each player's wooden followers on it, so majorities are engineered by merging features late with a spare meeple.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.finite_supply: Each player has exactly seven followers for the whole game; a meeple committed to a feature returns only when that feature completes, and farmers stay pinned until final scoring.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco: Points land on the scoring track the moment a road, city, or cloister completes, so mid-game standings are public and tempo scoring competes with long-term farm investment.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: After the last tile, incomplete features score reduced values (one point per city tile and pennant, one per road tile, one per cloister-adjacent tile) and all fields are resolved.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The end condition is material exhaustion: the game finishes immediately after the player who places the final tile of the 72-tile supply completes their turn.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.weighted_decks: The 72-tile stack is a fixed, deliberately skewed distribution (many road and city connectors, few cloisters and rare junctions), so blind draws follow designed odds.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: Because the exact tile distribution is printed in the rules, strong players count what has appeared to judge whether the curve or crossroads needed to finish a city still exists.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.tile_reveal: The map does not exist in advance: the landscape emerges one random tile per turn from a single starting tile, so the board itself is the game's central shared artifact.
vector_explanations.turns.order.fixed_clockwise: Play strictly rotates clockwise with one draw-place-deploy turn per player and no rounds, phases, interrupts, or turn-order manipulation of any kind.

title: Ticket to Ride
resolution_core: collect train-card sets to claim map routes and complete secret tickets

vector_explanations.logistics.connection.route_claiming: A route is claimed by discarding a matching set of train cards, one per space in the route's color (any single color for gray routes), then placing your plastic trains on it forever.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.route_drafting: Destination tickets are drafted blind: you draw three and must keep at least one (two of three at setup), accepting secret contracts that will score or penalize you at game end.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.set_collection: Turn-to-turn play is accumulating same-color card sets sized to target routes, balancing the tempo cost of drawing against the risk that a rival grabs the link first.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.longest_road: The Longest Continuous Path bonus card awards 10 points at game end to the player with the longest unbroken chain of their own trains, counting each plastic train once.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.set_collection: Train cards in eight colors plus wild locomotives are the game's only currency, and every meaningful play is cashing a collected monochrome set for board position.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.contract_fulfillment: Each destination ticket names two cities and a point value; connecting them with a continuous line of your trains banks those points when tickets are revealed at the end.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.route_building: Players assemble personal rail networks link by link on a shared North American map, where every claimed route is both connectivity for you and a wall for everyone else.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.connection_goals: Tickets like Los Angeles-New York City (21 points) are explicit location-pair goals, and the whole spatial puzzle is threading multiple tickets through shared corridors.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.connection_sharing_rules: Double routes codify sharing: parallel tracks let two different players connect the same cities, but in games with two or three players only one track of each pair may be used.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_replenishment: Refilling is a full action: draw two train cards per turn from the face-up row or the blind deck, so hand growth always competes with claiming routes and drawing tickets.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: There is no hand limit, and hoarding for long routes versus spending early to block is the core tension, since cards in hand are worth nothing until converted to track.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hidden_hand_information: Train cards drawn blind and tickets are private, so opponents infer your targets from which face-up colors you take and where your trains start appearing.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.open_display_draft: Five train cards sit face up beside the deck; taking a face-up locomotive costs your entire draw, and three locomotives showing at once forces the whole display to be discarded and refreshed.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.network_length_scoring: Route points scale superlinearly with length on a fixed table (1, 2, 4, 7, 10, 15 for one through six spaces), so long routes are far more point-efficient per train.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: Destination tickets stay secret until final scoring, hiding true standings; a trailing player may actually be leading on unrevealed 20-point transcontinental tickets.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: Every uncompleted destination ticket subtracts its full printed value at game end, making late ticket draws a push-your-luck gamble against remaining track supply.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: When any player's train supply drops to two or fewer plastic trains at the end of a turn, every player including that one gets exactly one final turn before scoring.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Final accounting reveals all tickets, adds or subtracts their values, and awards the 10-point Longest Continuous Path bonus on top of route points scored during play.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: With a fixed deck of 12 cards per color plus 14 locomotives, tracking what has been claimed and discarded sharpens estimates of whether your needed color can still be assembled.
vector_explanations.turns.order.fixed_clockwise: Turns rotate clockwise and each is exactly one of three actions: draw train cards, claim a route, or draw destination tickets, with no phases or reactions.

title: Puerto Rico
resolution_core: role selection with shared phases and chooser privileges

vector_explanations.actions.selection.role_selection: On your pick you take one of the face-up roles (Settler, Mayor, Builder, Craftsman, Trader, Captain, Prospector); everyone performs its action but you keep the printed privilege.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.role_selected_phases: Rounds have no fixed phase order: the round is literally the sequence of roles chosen, so a round without a Craftsman pick simply has no production phase at all.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.stale_action_bonuses: Roles left unchosen at round's end each gain a doubloon from the bank, so neglected roles like Prospector or Settler steadily sweeten until someone takes them.
vector_explanations.economy.production.action_role_selection: The Craftsman produces goods for every player with staffed plantations and processing buildings, but the chooser alone takes one extra good of a type they produced.
vector_explanations.economy.market.worker_station_allocation: Colonists occupy circular slots on plantations and buildings and only staffed slots function; the Mayor phase redistributes your entire workforce across those stations.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.colonist_movement: New colonists arrive on the colonist ship, which refills to the number of empty building slots in play, and the Mayor distributes them with the chooser taking one extra from the supply.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.plantation_farmin: Plantation tiles for corn, indigo, sugar, tobacco and coffee produce only when a colonist stands on them, and all but corn also need a matching staffed processing building.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.production_chain: Raw crops chain into barrels through paired infrastructure: an indigo plantation feeds an indigo plant, sugar feeds the mill, so production requires both ends staffed.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.tableau_engine: Your island board and city grid form a personal tableau whose plantations, small buildings and violet large buildings compound into a recurring production and scoring engine.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.sell_action: The Trader phase sells one good per player to the trading house at fixed prices from corn's 0 to coffee's 4, with the constraint that the house's four slots must hold different goods.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.ship_shipping: In the Captain phase players must ship if legally able, each cargo ship carries exactly one good type, and the Captain privilege pays one bonus VP for the first shipment.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.storage_limits: Spoilage rules bite after shipping: each player keeps only one leftover good, plus whatever warehouses protect, and everything else returns to the supply unscored.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.finite_supply: The finite stocks of colonists and victory point chips are explicit game clocks: when either supply empties, the game ends after the current round completes.
vector_explanations.turns.order.rotating_start_player: The Governor card passes clockwise each round, rotating who picks a role first and therefore who steers the round's phase order and captures the best privileges.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.seat_order_compensation: Starting plantations compensate seating: earlier players begin with indigo while the later seats receive corn, the only crop that produces without a processing building.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.hidden_score_tracking: Victory point chips earned from shipping are kept face down, so the shipping side of everyone's score is hidden and leader-bashing relies on estimates.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco: Shipping pays out instantly at one VP chip per barrel loaded during the Captain phase, so tempo players bank points continuously rather than waiting for the final tally.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: Three independent conditions end the game after the round: the colonist supply runs out, the VP chips run out, or any player fills the twelfth space of their city grid.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Final scoring adds printed building values and the large buildings' bonus formulas (Guild Hall, Residence, Fortress, Customs House, City Hall) to accumulated shipping chips.

title: Power Grid
resolution_core: power plant auctions + supply-track resource buying + paid network expansion

vector_explanations.economy.auction.open_outcry_auction: A player nominates a plant from the current market at a minimum bid of its printed number, then bidding rises freely around the table until all but one bidder has passed.
vector_explanations.economy.market.card_drafting: The plant market shows eight plants sorted by number: only the cheapest four (the current market) may be auctioned while the other four preview the future, and sold plants are refilled from the deck.
vector_explanations.economy.market.supply_demand_tracks: Coal, oil, garbage and uranium sit on a priced track where plentiful stock occupies cheap slots; every purchase empties cheap spaces and pushes the going price upward.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resource_purchasing: Fuel is never produced by players, only bought from the market track in reverse player order, so the leader buys last and pays the prices the trailers have driven up.
vector_explanations.economy.production.coal: Coal is the workhorse commodity of the early plant deck, and its market price swings hardest because cheap hybrid and coal plants make everyone draw on the same stock.
vector_explanations.economy.production.energy: Plants convert stored fuel into electricity during Bureaucracy: each fired plant burns its printed fuel cost to power its printed number of cities, and unfired fuel stays warehoused on the plant.
vector_explanations.economy.production.power_cities: Income is read off a payment chart by the number of cities you actually power this round, not the number you own, so plant capacity and fuel logistics cap your paycheck.
vector_explanations.economy.finance.hidden_money: Elektro is kept hidden behind screens of hands by explicit rule, so auction aggression and endgame building power must be estimated from observed spending.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.network_connections: Your grid grows city by city, and each expansion pays the city's slot price plus the printed connection cost of the cheapest path from your existing network.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.connection_sharing_rules: Cities have three slots priced 10, 15 and 20 Elektro, and the second and third slots only open in Steps 2 and 3, so early birds lock rivals out of whole regions.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.network_building: Every new city must be reachable from your current network by paying summed connection fees, making route topology and chokepoint denial central to expansion planning.
vector_explanations.turns.order.last_becomes_first: Player order is re-sorted every round by cities connected (ties by highest plant number), and key phases run in reverse order so the weakest position acts first.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.catch_up_mechanisms: Trailing players buy resources and build cities before the leaders, systematically getting cheaper fuel and first pick of contested city slots as a structural subsidy.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.runaway_leader_mitigatio: Leading is taxed on every axis: the leader nominates and bids from the worst position, buys fuel at inflated prices, and builds last into a partially claimed map.
vector_explanations.scoring.balance.tiebreakers: If players tie on cities powered at game end, the explicit tiebreaker is remaining money, which rewards efficient spending in the final buying frenzy.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Every round runs five fixed phases in order: determine player order, auction power plants, buy resources, build into cities, and Bureaucracy (income, restock, market adjustment).
vector_explanations.actions.timing.pass_and_out: Passing is binding: declining to nominate a plant removes you from the entire auction phase, and passing on a specific auction bars you from re-entering bidding for that plant.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The endgame fires when any player connects the trigger number of cities (14 to 21 depending on player count); the winner is whoever can then power the most cities.

title: Agricola
resolution_core: worker placement on escalating action spaces punctuated by harvest feeding

vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_placement: Each round players alternate placing family members one at a time onto action spaces, immediately resolving the space; you start with two workers and thirty-plus spaces open up over the game.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.blocked_spaces: Every action space holds exactly one family member per round (bar a few multi-player family-version spaces), so each placement is simultaneously an action and a denial.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.accumulating_action_sp: Untaken accumulation spaces like Wood, Clay, Reed and Fishing gain fresh goods every round, growing into stockpiles that eventually tempt someone into an off-plan detour.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_pool_growth: Family Growth adds a new person token who works from the next round onward, but only if you have built room, so worker expansion is gated by house construction.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_feeding_upkeep: Every worker is a mouth: harvests demand two food per adult and one per newborn, so each Family Growth permanently raises the tax on your food engine.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.feeding_family: Feeding is the game's whip: at each of the six harvests your whole family must be fed, and every missing food forces a Begging card worth minus three points.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.food_tokens: Food is a discrete token currency earned from fishing, baking, cooking and day-labouring, hoarded between harvests and audited to the unit at feeding time.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.upkeep_maintenance_phase: Harvest is a mandatory maintenance sequence with fixed sub-steps: reap one grain or vegetable per sown field, feed the family, then breed animals.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.seasonal_cycle_structure: The 14 rounds group into six stages ending in harvests after rounds 4, 7, 9, 11, 13 and 14, and the shrinking gaps between harvests accelerate the feeding pressure.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.round_phase_structure: Each round follows a fixed frame: reveal the new round card, replenish accumulation spaces, place and resolve workers in turn, then bring everyone home.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.turn_order_selection: A dedicated Starting Player action space lets a worker claim the first-player marker for the following round, a placement veterans buy before contested Family Growth rounds.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.harvest_cycles: Fields sown with grain or vegetables pay out one unit per field at every harvest until emptied, so sowing timing against the harvest calendar is a core planning axis.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.farming: Plough Field, Sow, and Bake Bread are separate action spaces forming an explicit agronomic pipeline from empty farmyard squares to grain and food.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.animal_breeding: At each harvest, every animal species of which you hold at least two breeds exactly one newborn, provided you have capacity to house it, rewarding kept breeding pairs over eaten ones.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.fencing: The Fences action space builds wooden fences that partition farmyard squares into pastures, and pasture size (doubled by stables) strictly caps animal capacity.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.room_renovation: The Renovation action upgrades all rooms of your house at once from wood to clay to stone, worth escalating points and a prerequisite for several improvements.
vector_explanations.economy.production.agricultural_goods: Grain, vegetables, sheep, boar and cattle are distinct scored goods, each with its own acquisition spaces and each convertible to food through the right improvement.
vector_explanations.economy.production.wood_clay_stone: Wood, clay, reed and stone are gathered from accumulation spaces and spent in strict recipes: rooms cost five wood or clay plus two reed, stables two wood, fences one wood each.
vector_explanations.economy.management.resource_allocation: With two to five workers against dozens of needs (rooms, food, fields, fences, growth), the whole game is triaging a permanently over-committed labor budget.
vector_explanations.economy.card.engine_cards: Each player is dealt seven Occupation and seven Minor Improvement cards at setup; played to your farm, cards like Hearth Cook or Basket grant permanent private rule-benders.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.upgrade_conversi: Major Improvements are converters: fireplaces and cooking hearths turn animals and vegetables into food at printed rates, while ovens convert grain into big bread payouts.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: The round cards are shuffled within each stage, so when key actions like Sow or Family Growth surface varies by game, as do the fourteen private cards you are dealt.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: The scoring pad punishes gaps: minus one point per unused farmyard square, per missing category (no sheep, no grain), and minus three per Begging card taken.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Final points come from seventeen-odd capped categories, each scoring minus one to four, so victory requires broad, balanced development rather than one maximized track.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Nothing scores until the game ends after round 14's harvest, when the category grid, card points, and bonus points from improvements are tallied in one pass.

title: Le Havre
resolution_core: take an offer pile or enter one building; production chains under rising food demand

vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.production_chain: The economy is a converter web: wood becomes charcoal, iron becomes steel, cattle become meat and hides, and mastering multi-step chains into ships is how fortunes compound.
vector_explanations.economy.production.goods_upgrade: Dedicated buildings refine basics into premium goods, such as the Smokehouse turning fish into smoked fish and the Charcoal Kiln turning wood into charcoal, roughly doubling value per step.
vector_explanations.economy.production.energy: Goods carry printed energy values (wood 1, coal 3, coke 10) and big plays like building ships at the Wharf demand paying energy alongside materials.
vector_explanations.economy.production.wood_clay_stone: Wood, clay, iron and their refined forms are spent in recipe-like building costs, so construction planning is matching material mixes to the building cards you covet.
vector_explanations.economy.finance.loan_interest: Loan cards hand you 4 francs on demand and are forced when you cannot feed; outstanding loans bleed interest at reckoning time and settle at a punishing premium against your final score.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.coin: Francs are simultaneously money and victory points at one-to-one, so every purchase is literally spending score and every fee paid to a rival is a two-way swing.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.sell_action: The Shipping Line building is the payoff valve: pay energy, load goods aboard your ships up to their capacities, and convert cargo into francs at each good's printed sale value.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.blocked_spaces: Your single worker disc enters exactly one building per turn, and an occupied building is unusable by anyone else until its visitor leaves, making occupancy itself a weapon.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.accumulating_action_sp: Each turn the ship marker adds two goods to the seven offer spaces, so piles of fish, wood, clay and francs swell until a player spends their whole turn scooping one pile.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.player_built_action_sp: Buildings that players construct become public action spaces: anyone may enter one you own, but they pay you the printed entry fee in francs or food for the privilege.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.upkeep_maintenance_phase: Each round closes with a feeding reckoning whose food demand rises steeply over the game, offset by owned ships that supply food automatically every round.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: A round is exactly seven player turns marked by the ship token's advance, after which the round card flips to trigger harvest, feeding, and any special event.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The number of rounds is fixed and printed for each player count, so the full arc of feeding demands and the final buying frenzy can be planned from turn one.
vector_explanations.logistics.survival.food_tokens: Food is a counted commodity distinct from francs, drawn from fish, bread and meat, and the escalating end-of-round food bill is the engine everyone builds against.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.harvest_cycles: At harvest, any player with at least one grain receives an extra grain and any player with at least two cattle receives a calf, making early livestock and grain investments self-multiply.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.animal_breeding: Cattle growth needs a breeding pair: two or more cattle at harvest yield exactly one additional head, which the Abattoir later converts into meat and valuable hides.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: The special buildings entering play are drawn from a larger deck and the three building proposal stacks are ordered variably, so available combos shift every game.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: After the final round each player may take one last action, then scores are totalled as cash plus the printed franc values of all owned buildings and ships.

title: Terra Mystica
resolution_core: terraform-and-build engine with power-bowl economy and cult tracks

vector_explanations.economy.production.power_bowl_cycle: Purple power tokens cycle through three bowls: gains move tokens from Bowl I to II to III, only Bowl III power is spendable, and burning (destroying one token to promote another) buys speed at a cost.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resources: The engine runs on four currencies with different plumbing: workers dig and build, coins pay upgrades and power actions, priests feed cults, and power fuels the special actions.
vector_explanations.economy.production.income_generation: The income phase pays workers, coins, priests and power according to slots uncovered on your faction board, so every structure built permanently raises next round's inflow.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.terrain_placement_rest: Dwellings may only be built on your faction's home terrain, so expansion means paying spades to spin hexes around the seven-color terraforming wheel toward your color first.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.starting_position_draf: Placing the two starting dwellings in alternating order is a draft that fixes your whole game's geography, since all later growth must stay adjacent to what you seeded.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.adjacency_bonuses: Direct adjacency cuts both ways: building next to rivals halves your trading house upgrade cost from six coins to three, but it also offers them leech power for your build.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.network_connections: Endgame area scoring counts your largest group of connected buildings, with bridges and shipping-linked river hexes counting as connections, so sprawl must eventually be stitched together.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.shipping_range: River hexes are not buildable and count against reach: your shipping value sets how many river spaces you may skip when determining indirect adjacency for building.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.shipping_upgrade: A dedicated action spends a priest and coins to advance your shipping track for immediate victory points and permanently longer reach across the river network.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.upgrade_actions: Structures upgrade along a fixed tree - dwelling to trading house to stronghold or temple, temple to sanctuary - swapping pieces on the map and unlocking bigger income and powers.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.temple_tracks: Priests are committed to the Fire, Water, Earth and Air cult tracks; thresholds pay power, crossing to 10 needs a town key, and each track pays 8/4/2 points to its leaders at game end.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: Fourteen factions across seven terrain colors bend different rules: Darklings dig with priests, Fakirs jump deserts by carpet, Swarmlings get outsized towns, Nomads start with three dwellings.
vector_explanations.economy.card.privileges_scrolls: Favor tiles earned by building temples grant cult steps plus permanent privileges such as extra income or the town-founding threshold dropping from seven to six power value.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_drafting: The six public power actions (bridge, priest, workers, coins, spades) are octagonal once-per-round spaces that deplete as players claim them, so timing beats price.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.stale_action_bonuses: Bonus cards not chosen during passing accumulate one coin per round, so a mediocre card gathers a bribe until its side pot makes someone bite.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.pass_and_stay_out: Turns continue around the table until each player passes out of the round individually, so lean plans get fewer actions but choose their exit and next-round bonus card early.
vector_explanations.logistics.turn_order.pass_order: Passing first claims the start player marker for the following round, so a deliberately short round buys initiative for contested spades and power actions.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game is exactly six rounds of income, actions and cult bonuses, and the six visible round scoring tiles let players plan the entire arc from setup.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.variable_scoring_condi: Round scoring tiles drawn at setup pay immediate points for specific acts (two per dwelling, five per stronghold) and hand out cult-threshold rewards at round end, reshaping each game's tempo.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.majority_rankings: Final scoring ranks players twice: 18/12/6 points for the largest connected building areas and 8/4/2 on every cult track, with ties splitting the pooled points.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.leftover_resource_conv: At game end leftover workers, priests and power convert to coins and every three coins become one victory point, so even the scraps of your engine are audited.

title: Gaia Project
resolution_core: terraform-and-settle engine with power cycling and six research tracks

vector_explanations.simulation.environment.terraforming_gaia: Planets cost ore per terraforming step scaled by their distance from your home type on the color wheel, while purple Transdim planets need a Gaiaformer and a full Gaia phase to become settleable Gaia planets.
vector_explanations.economy.production.power_bowl_cycle: The three-bowl power loop returns from Terra Mystica with a twist: tokens committed to Gaiaformers detour through the green Gaia area before re-entering Bowl I.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resources: Ore, credits, knowledge and QICs each have distinct sinks - ore terraforms and builds, knowledge climbs research, QICs buy range and special actions - so conversion timing is the game.
vector_explanations.economy.production.income_generation: Income scales with your board state: uncovered mine, trading station and lab slots plus research levels and boosters pay ore, credits, knowledge and power charges every round.
vector_explanations.simulation.technology.research_board: Knowledge is spent to climb six research tracks (Terraforming, Navigation, AI, Gaia Project, Economy, Science); level five of each track is capped at a single player and demands flipping a green federation token.
vector_explanations.politics.factions.asymmetrical_abilities: Fourteen factions warp core systems: Terrans recycle Gaia-phase power into resources, Ivits build one growing space station network, and the Bescods swap their building income scheme entirely.
vector_explanations.exploration.mapping.modular_boards: The galaxy is assembled from ten double-hex sector tiles that can be arranged and rotated freely, so planet distances and color scarcity differ radically between plays.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.terrain_placement_rest: Mines may only be founded on planets matching your home type after terraforming, and every build must be within range of an existing structure or paid for with QIC-extended reach.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.starting_position_draf: Initial mines are placed in a structured draft in and against turn order, and seeding near contested planet clusters trades safety for later leech and federation potential.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.network_connections: Federations form when connected structures total seven power value, with power tokens spent as satellites to bridge empty space, paying a federation token of points and resources.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.shipping_range: Basic range is two hexes from your nearest structure; distant planets require spending QICs for temporary range boosts, making reach a priced resource on every build.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.shipping_upgrade: Climbing the Navigation research track permanently raises base range and eventually awards the Lost Planet, converting knowledge into lasting mobility.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.upgrade_actions: Buildings upgrade along a fixed tree - mine to trading station to planetary institute, or trading station to research lab to academy - each step trading credits and ore for stronger income and powers.
vector_explanations.economy.card.privileges_scrolls: Standard and advanced tech tiles grant permanent income, one-shot bursts, or endgame scoring privileges, and advanced tiles must be earned with federation tokens and high track positions.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_drafting: The public power and QIC actions beneath the research board are once-per-round octagons that deplete as players claim them, so the cheap terraforming and knowledge grabs go to whoever acts first.
vector_explanations.turns.variance.pass_and_stay_out: Players keep alternating single actions until each passes individually, so action-efficient engines squeeze extra turns out of every round while spent players watch.
vector_explanations.logistics.turn_order.pass_order: The order in which players pass fully determines next round's turn order, making an early pass a purchasable initiative advantage rather than a mere concession.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: Exactly six rounds of income, Gaia phase, actions and cleanup structure the game, with all six round scoring tiles visible from setup for long-range planning.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.variable_scoring_condi: Each game deals random round scoring tiles plus two final scoring tiles (most planet types, most sectors, largest federated network...), redefining what this particular galaxy rewards.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.majority_rankings: The two final scoring categories award 18/12/6 points by rank, with a neutral dummy ranking inserted in games with fewer players to keep majorities honest.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.leftover_resource_conv: At game end research levels above two pay four points each and leftover ore, credits and knowledge convert three-to-one into points, so terminal stockpiles are salvaged.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Most points arrive in the final audit: final scoring tiles, research track positions, resource conversion and banked federation tokens land on top of in-game scoring-tile points.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: The official Automa opponent plays from a card deck that mimics faction pressure, taking planets and passing at a tuned tempo across selectable difficulty levels.

title: Concordia
resolution_core: hand of Roman action cards played one per turn, recovered en masse by the Tribune

vector_explanations.economy.card.deck_building_hand: Bought personality cards join your hand rather than a shuffled deck, so your action repertoire grows deterministically and every acquisition is immediately playable.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.action_cards_hand: A turn is playing exactly one card from hand - Architect, Prefect, Mercator, Senator, Diplomat, Tribune - and the played card lies exhausted in your personal discard row.
vector_explanations.actions.selection.exhaust_and_refresh_ac: Spent cards only return when you play the Tribune, which takes back your whole discard row, pays a sestertius per card beyond three, and may deploy a colonist for food and tools.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Sequencing is everything: stringing Architect-Prefect-Mercator runs before the Tribune reset, while the Diplomat lets you copy the top card of a rival's discard row.
vector_explanations.economy.market.card_drafting: New personality cards are bought by the Senator from a public row of seven slots whose position surcharges make fresh arrivals expensive and lingering cards progressively cheaper.
vector_explanations.economy.production.city_goods: Every city on the map produces one signature good - brick, food, tools, wine or cloth - so where you build houses fixes your production portfolio for the whole game.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resource_sharing: The Prefect activates an entire province: every player's houses in its cities produce, while the prefect player additionally pockets the province's bonus marker.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resources: Brick, food, tools, wine and cloth are the five goods, each needed somewhere specific: brick and food build houses, food and tools deploy colonists, cloth funds the priciest cities.
vector_explanations.economy.market.resource_pricing: Goods have fixed market values of 3, 4, 5, 6 and 7 sestertii from brick up to cloth, an official price list that governs Mercator trades and Vesta's endgame valuation.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.sell_action: The Mercator grants three or five sestertii and then lets you buy and sell any two good types at the printed prices, making it the game's liquidity engine.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: New land and sea colonists are recruited by paying one food and one tool via the Tribune or Colonist card, expanding both your movement network and your Mars scoring base.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.storage_limits: Your storehouse has exactly twelve cells and colonists in reserve occupy cells alongside goods, so a bloated warehouse literally crowds out your workforce.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Land colonists walk the road segments and sea colonists sail the sea lines between cities, and the Architect moves your colonists a total number of steps equal to your colonist count.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.colonist_movement: Where your colonists stand determines everything the Architect can do, since houses may only be built in cities adjacent to one of your land or sea colonists.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: City tokens are shuffled and dealt to the provinces at setup, randomizing which cities make which goods and therefore where the wine and cloth rush will happen.
vector_explanations.turns.order.fixed_clockwise: There are no rounds or phases: play is a fixed clockwise sequence of single card plays until an end condition arrives, plus one final turn for everyone but the finisher.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.set_scoring_multiplier: Every card names a god and scores its formula again per copy: Vesta values wealth, Jupiter pays per non-brick city, Saturnus per province settled, Mercurius per good type produced, Mars per deployed colonist.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: The game ends when a player builds their fifteenth house or buys the last personality card; the finisher claims the seven-point Concordia card and everyone else takes one last turn.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: All scoring happens in the final god-by-god audit, where board development is multiplied through the cards accumulated in hand and discard row alike.

title: The Castles of Burgundy
resolution_core: two dice per turn drive tile drafting and placement on a personal estate

vector_explanations.actions.selection.dice_action_pools: Each turn you roll two dice and each die performs one action constrained by its pips: draft from the matching numbered depot, place onto a matching numbered hex, sell goods, or take workers.
vector_explanations.luck.mitigation.resource_to_modify_dice: Worker tokens are the official dice fixer: each worker spent adjusts one die by plus or minus one, wrapping between six and one, and the take-workers action grants two.
vector_explanations.actions.points.free_actions: Beyond the two die actions, once per turn you may pay two silverlings to buy any tile from the central black depot, a money-powered escape valve from dice constraints.
vector_explanations.economy.card.tile_token_drafting: Hex tiles are drafted from six numbered open depots refilled each phase, so every acquisition is a public pick that also denies neighbors a visible option.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.storage_limits: Drafted tiles go to a three-slot staging area on your board before placement, and overflow is impossible: with all three slots full you must place before you can draft again.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.coin: Silverlings are the scarce cash earned from ship goods and mines (one per mine at each phase's end), spent almost exclusively on the black-depot free action.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.sell_action: A die action sells every goods tile of one stored type, paying one silverling plus victory points per tile scaled by player count, so batching sales is rewarded.
vector_explanations.economy.card.privileges_scrolls: Yellow knowledge tiles are permanent private rule-benders: individual tiles loosen placement restrictions, boost sales, cheapen workers, or add endgame bonuses for specific tile types.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.personal_map_building: Each player fills a private 37-hex duchy whose printed terrain layout is scored independently, so the spatial puzzle is yours alone even while drafting is contested.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.terrain_placement_rest: A tile must match its space twice over: ships only on blue river hexes, animals on green pastures, and every placement must land on a space whose printed number matches the die and touch an already filled hex.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.grid_coverage_bonuses: Filling the last hex of a color-contiguous region scores immediately: points scale with region size, plus a completion bonus that shrinks from ten in phase A to two in phase E, rewarding early finishes.
vector_explanations.simulation.building.tile_activation: Every placed tile fires on placement: castles grant a bonus die action, ships collect goods and advance turn order, buildings trigger their printed one-shot effects, mines and knowledge run passively.
vector_explanations.turns.order.turn_order_selection_track: Turn order is a track manipulated in play: placing ship tiles advances your marker, and acting earlier next round means first pick of freshly dealt depot tiles.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game is exactly five phases of five rounds each, twenty-five turns per player, with the dwindling phase letters visibly counting down the completion-bonus clock.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Each phase opens with a full restock ritual: depots are refilled with random hex tiles and five new goods tiles are distributed before the five rounds of dice turns begin.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Which hex tiles surface in which depots each phase is random, and advanced play deals differing 37-hex estate layouts, so no two duchies reward the same plan.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.immediate_incremental_sco: Nearly all points land mid-game the moment regions complete or goods sell, so the score track is a live standings board that informs blocking and drafting.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: Being first to fill every hex of an entire color across your duchy claims the larger first-come bonus tile for that color, with a smaller tile for the runner-up.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Points trickle from everywhere - region completions, goods sales, knowledge bonuses, color-completion tiles, leftover workers and silverlings - the archetypal Feld point salad.

title: Brass: Birmingham
resolution_core: card-constrained builds on a shared network across canal and rail eras

vector_explanations.logistics.connection.canal_links: In the canal era players build canal links for three pounds each, and at the era's end all canals plus every level-1 industry are wiped from the board as obsolete.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.rail_links: Rail-era links consume infrastructure: a single rail costs five pounds plus a coal cube, while laying two rails at once costs fifteen pounds, a coal for each, and a beer barrel.
vector_explanations.spatial.network.network_connections: Your link network defines legality everywhere: you may only build in or adjacent to your network (or on cards' named locations), and era scoring pays each link for the flipped tiles around it.
vector_explanations.economy.market.income_track: Income is a marker on a bent track: sold industries push it up, loans drag it down three levels, and every round you collect (or pay, below zero) your current income in cash.
vector_explanations.economy.finance.loan_interest: The Loan action grants thirty pounds but permanently lowers your income marker by three levels, a priced debt that can drive income negative and never gets repaid.
vector_explanations.economy.production.beer: Breweries produce beer barrels that selling manufactured goods consumes; barrels can come from your own breweries, connected rival breweries, or the merchant's own barrel for its bonus.
vector_explanations.economy.production.coal: Coal mines place cubes on their tiles and coal moves only along built links, so a mine without connections is stranded while the coal market backstops shortages at rising prices.
vector_explanations.economy.production.iron: Ironworks cubes, unlike coal, may be consumed from anywhere on the board without a connection, an asymmetry that makes iron a boardwide public utility.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resources_on_map: Coal, iron and beer live as physical cubes and barrels on the industry tiles that made them, and consuming the last cube flips that tile for its owner - friend or rival.
vector_explanations.economy.market.supply_demand_tracks: The coal and iron markets are priced tracks that empty upward and refill downward: buying from a bare market is expensive, while selling surplus into a full one pays little.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.sell_action: The Sell action requires a link to a merchant tile that demands your good type and a beer per tile sold; meeting both flips the manufactory and banks its income and points.
vector_explanations.economy.engine_building.triggered_income: Tiles flip when their resources are consumed by anyone, so a rival buying your coal or beer advances your income marker off-turn - being exploited is profitable.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: You hold eight cards and every action discards one; location cards build anywhere in your network while industry cards build only that industry, so the hand is a straitjacket to plan around.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.card_as_currency: Cards are spent as fuel: any card may be discarded face down to pay for Loan, Develop, Network or Sell actions, and the Scout action burns three cards for two wilds.
vector_explanations.actions.points.action_point_allowance: Turns are a strict budget of two actions (one in the canal era's first round) chosen from Build, Network, Develop, Sell, Loan, Scout and Pass.
vector_explanations.logistics.maintenance.upgrade_actions: The Develop action consumes an iron cube to remove one or two low-level tiles from your player mat, permanently unlocking higher-level, higher-scoring industry builds.
vector_explanations.logistics.turn_order.spending_based: Next round's turn order is sorted by money spent this round, ascending, so frugal turns buy initiative while big spending rounds push you to the back.
vector_explanations.spatial.placement.overbuilding: Explicit overbuild rules let you replace your own industry with a higher level, and replace a rival's coal or iron tile only when that resource is exhausted both on the board and in its market.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: Each era lasts a fixed number of rounds set by the deck: cards are dealt to a known count, the draw pile visibly shrinks, and both eras end exactly when hands run dry.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.triggered_scoring_rounds: Scoring fires twice, triggered by each era's deck exhausting: links score first and are removed, then every flipped industry tile pays its printed victory points.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.randomized_setup_variabi: Merchant tiles are drawn and placed randomly at the map's edge ports each setup, changing which goods the external market demands and where the beer-hungry Sell runs will flow.

title: Great Western Trail
resolution_core: deckbuilt cattle hand delivered via repeated trail circuits to Kansas City

vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.deck_cycling_tempo: Your cattle deck cycles constantly: buildings discard and draw cards, and engineering when your best breeds return to hand before a Kansas City delivery is the game's signature skill.
vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.starter_deck_upgrades: Everyone starts with an identical fourteen-card herd of low-value breeds worth one and two, and the arc of the game is diluting those cheap cows with purchased premium cattle.
vector_explanations.economy.card.deck_building: Cattle bought at the market shuffle into your personal draw deck, so herd purchases are deck-building decisions about density, breed diversity and cycling speed.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.cattle_market: The cattle market sells graded breeds - value-three Brown Swiss and Ayrshire up to value-five Texas Longhorn - paid with money and the labor of your hired cowboys.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_limits: Your hand is capped at four cattle cards until discs removed from your player board raise the limit stepwise toward six, directly throttling the herd value a delivery can show.
vector_explanations.cards.collection.set_collection: A delivery's herd value sums only distinct breeds in hand, duplicates count nothing, so breed variety rather than raw quantity is what Kansas City pays for.
vector_explanations.logistics.travel.trail_movement: Movement follows the one-way trail from the south toward Kansas City in steps capped by your movement rating, and each stop's building grants that turn's actions.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.terrain_movement_modifi: Hazard and teepee tiles occupy trail spaces, count as steps, and hand icons on them and on rival buildings levy crossing fees, so the trail's cost profile shifts all game.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.player_built_action_sp: Craftsmen erect your private building tiles on empty trail spaces, creating owner-only action stops whose hand icons toll passing opponents for your benefit.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: The job market sells cowboys, craftsmen and engineers whose hiring cost rises row by row as the market fills, and each hire permanently strengthens one action type.
vector_explanations.economy.production.station_master: When upgrading a station you may promote a worker to station master there, claiming an immediate bonus tile and endgame points tied to that station's role.
vector_explanations.economy.production.enhancements: Removing discs from your player board is the enhancement engine: each removal permanently unlocks a bigger hand, longer moves, better certificates or extra auxiliary actions.
vector_explanations.logistics.connection.shipping_range: Your locomotive's position on the shared track caps which cities you can deliver to without paying the shortfall, and engineers push the engine ahead to extend penalty-free range.
vector_explanations.cards.drafting.constrained_selection_dra: The Kansas City foresight phase reveals two tiles in each of three slots - hazards, teepees, workers - and you must pick exactly one from each pair to enter the board.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.hidden_objectives: Objective cards shuffle into your cattle deck; playing one in front of you commits to its plus-or-minus evaluation, while objectives left inside the deck can only ever score positively.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.negative_point_penalti: Committed objectives you fail to satisfy score their printed negative value, and weak deliveries can force discs onto penalty city spaces, so overreach is priced.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Final points pour from a dozen tracks: money, buildings, delivered-city discs, stations, station masters, hazards collected, workers hired, objectives and high-value cattle.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_triggers: Workers entering the job market are the clock: when the marker reaches the market's final row, the end is triggered and players finish with a last Kansas City run.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Almost everything scores in the final audit against the scoring pad, which is why mid-game standings in GWT are notoriously unreadable at the table.

title: Viticulture: Essential Edition
resolution_core: seasonal worker placement converting grapes into aged wine for orders

vector_explanations.economy.market.worker_placement_seasons: The board is split into summer and winter action zones: planting, building and tours happen in summer, while harvesting, winemaking and order-filling wait for winter.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_placement: Workers are placed one per turn onto action spaces around the vineyard board, and a placed worker both performs the action and squats the slot for the year.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.blocked_spaces: Each action offers a limited row of slots scaled to player count, so popular winter spaces like Make Wine and Fill Order jam up and reward acting early.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_types_upgrades: The oversized grande worker is a privileged type: it may be placed on a completely full action space, guaranteeing that one critical action can never be blocked out.
vector_explanations.actions.placement.worker_pool_growth: The winter Train Worker space converts four lira into a new worker for future years, growing your crew toward six and multiplying seasonal throughput.
vector_explanations.actions.timing.turn_order_selection: Each spring players pick positions on the wake-up chart, explicitly buying their spot in the year's turn order as an opening decision every single year.
vector_explanations.turns.order.turn_order_selection_track: Later wake-up rows compensate their slow start with printed perks - extra cards, a lira, a victory point, or a one-year temporary worker - pricing initiative honestly.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.seasonal_cycle_structure: Play is organized into years of spring, summer, fall and winter: wake-up in spring, summer placements, a visitor card drawn in fall, winter placements, then year-end cleanup and aging.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.wine_making: The full pipeline is modeled with dedicated spaces: plant vine cards in fields, harvest fields into crush pad grapes, combine grapes into red, white, blush or sparkling wines in the cellar.
vector_explanations.simulation.agriculture.farming: Vine cards carry planting requirements and field limits: some vines demand a trellis or irrigation before planting, and each of your three fields caps total vine value.
vector_explanations.economy.production.grape_aging: Grapes on the crush pad and wines in the cellar each gain one value at every year's end, so patience literally appreciates your inventory along the numbered tracks.
vector_explanations.economy.production.income_generation: Filled wine orders advance your residual income track, which pays out lira at the end of every subsequent year, converting past sales into a compounding annuity.
vector_explanations.economy.production.enhancements: Buildings are permanent efficiency unlocks: the trellis and irrigation gate premium vines, the windmill and tasting room drip victory points, and cellars extend aging.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.storage_limits: Wine value is capped by cellar infrastructure: values four through six require the medium cellar and seven through nine the large cellar, so aging without building stalls.
vector_explanations.cards.hand.hand_management: Summer and winter visitor cards are powerful one-shot effects, and choosing when to burn them - especially winter visitors that mimic scarce actions - drives tempo swings.
vector_explanations.cards.deck_manipulation.multiple_deck_so: Four distinct decks - vines, wine orders, summer visitors, winter visitors - are drawn from via different action spaces, so card acquisition is a choice of source profile.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.contract_fulfillment: Wine order cards demand specific wine types and values and pay printed victory points plus residual income when filled from your cellar, forming the game's main scoring artery.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.race_to_finish: The game ends with the year in which any player reaches twenty victory points, so the finish line is a sprint trigger rather than a fixed round count.
vector_explanations.cooperation.structure.solo_mode_design: The Essential Edition includes the Automa solo opponent by Morten Monrad Pedersen, whose card-driven placements block action spaces at tuned difficulty levels.

title: Orléans
resolution_core: bag building: draw followers from a curated bag onto follower-combo action spaces

vector_explanations.cards.deckbuilding.bag_building: The deck-building loop runs on cloth tokens in a bag: every follower you recruit goes into your bag, and each round you draw blind from whatever population you have curated.
vector_explanations.economy.card.bag_building_workers: Drawn followers are your workers for the round, placed onto action spaces; used followers return to the bag while some are permanently committed to buildings, boats or deeds.
vector_explanations.luck.generators.bag_building_draws: All in-round randomness comes from the bag draw, and knights raise your draw limit from four toward eight, letting heavy recruiters see more of their bag each round.
vector_explanations.luck.odds.card_counting_memory: Because you chose every token in your bag, expert play is draw-odds bookkeeping: knowing exactly how many boatmen remain undrawn tells you whether to plan the Ship this round.
vector_explanations.economy.trading.unit_recruitment: Dedicated spaces recruit each follower type into your bag - the Farm House hires farmers, the Village boatmen, craftsmen or traders, the University scholars, the Castle knights, the Monastery wildcard monks.
vector_explanations.economy.production.enhancements: Technology tiles are permanent efficiency upgrades: mounted on one slot of an action space, a tile counts forever as the follower it replaces, discounting that action each round.
vector_explanations.economy.market.resource_pricing: The five goods carry a fixed value ladder - grain one, cheese two, wool three, wine four, brocade five - governing both event levies and their worth at final scoring.
vector_explanations.economy.production.resources_on_map: Goods tiles are seeded onto the road and river segments of the France map at setup, and the first merchant to travel a laden route collects its goods token.
vector_explanations.economy.resources.coin: Coins pay hiring costs, tolls and tax events, and unspent money converts to points at game end, so the treasury is simultaneously operating capital and score.
vector_explanations.spatial.movement.point_to_point: Your merchant pawn travels the printed road and water routes between towns (wagon by land, boat by river), and trading stations may only be planted where the merchant stands.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.planning_then_execution_spl: Everyone plans simultaneously, secretly slotting drawn followers onto their action spaces behind a screen of options, then actions are revealed and resolved player by player.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.structured_round_phases: Every round marches through the same seven steps: reveal the hourglass event, census, draw followers, plan placements, execute actions, resolve the event, pass the start player.
vector_explanations.turns.phases.fixed_round_count_structure: The game always lasts exactly eighteen rounds, one per hourglass tile, so the number of bag draws remaining is public information from the very first turn.
vector_explanations.turns.order.fixed_clockwise: Within each execution phase players resolve their planned actions in clockwise order from the start player, whose marker rotates every round.
vector_explanations.simulation.environment.city_events: Each round's hourglass tile hits the whole table: Plague forces every player to lose one blind-drawn follower, Taxes levy coins per goods held, while Harvest and Income pay out.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.public_milestones: Citizen tiles are first-come prizes claimed from development-track milestones and from completing columns of the shared Beneficial Deeds board with donated followers.
vector_explanations.scoring.structure.victory_points_salad: Final scores stack coins, goods values and citizens plus trading stations multiplied by your development level, so engines, geography and civic play all pay.
vector_explanations.scoring.timing.end_game_scoring: Apart from coins gathered en route, everything converts at the end: the development multiplier makes identical stations and citizens worth wildly different totals per player.
